en.extract:
Burchell's courser (Cursorius rufus) is a wader in the pratincole and courser family, Glareolidae. The name of this bird commemorates the English naturalist William John Burchell.
Native to Africa, Burchell's courser is a small, diurnal, and terrestrial bird that lives in the western parts of southern Africa. Although classed as waders, these are birds of dry open country, preferably semi-desert, where they typically hunt their insect prey (usually Harvester Termites) by running on the ground. It principally feeds off of insects and lives in open, short grasslands and burnt veld. It grows up to 22 cm (8.7 in) and on average weighs about 75 g (2.6 oz) as an adult.


Description

Burchell's courser has a graceful figure and an upright posture. It has a blue-grey hind crown which is mainly how it differs from the similar Temminck's courser. It also has a horizontal black band demarcating a white underbelly, and an overall pale rufous colour. Its face features white supercilia above black eye lines, all ending at the back of the neck to form a double V. In terms of feathers, its secondaries are mostly white with a black underwing and brown to grey coverts. Typical of coursers, its bill is long, arched down, and of a dark colour like its eyes. Similar to the underbelly, the legs are of a distinctive creamy white. While flying, the Burchell's courser's feet extend past its tail. 
Females and males look similar, while juveniles lack the rufous colouring, the facial stripes, and the grey hind crown of the adults. They also are mottled, with black and beige barring. Overall, the Burchell's courser, with its camouflage appearance, blends in with the landscape making it challenging to spot.


Taxonomy
Burchell's courser belongs to the order Charadriiformes which mainly refers to birds living near water. Characterized by a curved beak and nostrils located at the base of the nose, the Burchell's courser is part of the Glareolidae family, specifically belonging to the courser group. Its genus, Cursorius, contains five species, all of which live in the arid parts of the Old World and display distinctive features such as long legs and short wings. 
Burchell's courser and its close relative, C. cursor, have at times been referred to as the same species. However, although they have a direct common ancestor, they should not be considered as such. C. rufus is monotypic and does not have any subspecies.


Distribution and habitat

Endemic to Africa, Burchell's courser prefers the warm and dry areas of Southern Africa. Specifically, it is found in most of South Africa and Namibia, and in parts of Angola (Southwest) and Botswana (Kalahari Basin). Its distribution range occasionally coincides with that of the Temminck's courser. 
Burchell's courser inhabits deserts or semi deserts with gravel rather than just sand, but also open grasslands with short grass, and sparse, burnt, or overgrazed vegetation. It is often referred as locally nomad based on its movements at the landscape level, possibly travelling due to fluctuations in rainfall. Although it is classified as least concern, Its population is declining, potentially due to its habitat loss on arable lands, inadequate farming practices such as intense irrigation and overuse of pesticides, and other anthropogenic disturbances.


Behaviour
The adults have developed a diversionary display as a strategy against predators. They perform "displacement brooding" to distract any potential threats from the nesting area. Even though the chicks are considered precocial, they lack the heat-loss mechanisms present in the adults to survive the hot climate, and thus need shade to avoid overheating. 


Voice

Burchell's courser often prefers to run rather than fly, and will most likely take to the air only when the disturbance is significant. Often referred to as inconspicuous, this bird is mostly quiet on the ground, but becomes vocal when flying, producing a loud flight call. Thus far, three types of vocalizations it produces have been identified: one throaty "chuk" as it launches in the air, a few "chuk chuk" grunts while flying, and a contact call consisting of a "kwirrt-kwirrt" or a "kok-kok-kwich". If threatened by a predator, the Burchell's courser may run away from its nest, emitting an alarm call in order to divert the predator.


Feeding
Burchell's courser is predominantly an insectivore. It uses its long bill to forage the ground and dig up insects. This bird also runs to catch its prey. It has been found to frequently eat from the Coleoptera group, especially curculionidae beetles as its main food source. However, the Burchell's courser tends to have a preference for harvester termites (Hodotermes mossambicus) when available, which can constitute over 50% of its diet. It almost never chooses seeds as a source of nutrition. The yearly frequency of rainfalls affects the food availability and quality, which is the main reason the Burchell's courser travels around.


Breeding
Burchell's coursers may occur in small flocks but are mostly solitary, monogamous birds. They have developed an all-year-round breeding strategy due to their nomadic movements, although they often breed just before the wet season between July and December.  
With a small clutch size, the Burchell's courser only lays two eggs directly on the ground, sometimes surrounded by stools of antelopes, small rocks, or dried up organic matter. The eggs have an oval shape, and look black from afar, but they are actually of a buff colour with many dark markings. There is a lot of uncertainty when it comes to the hatching and fledging times, but it is believed that both parents participate in the incubation period.


References


External links
Burchell's courser in The Atlas of Southern African Birds
Burchell's courser Cursorius rufus at The Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Burchell's courser photo gallery at VIREO
Burchell's courser photo gallery at eBird

'''Burchell's courser''' ('''''Cursorius rufus''''') is a [[wader]] in the [[pratincole]] and [[courser]] family, [[Glareolidae]].<ref name=Hancock>{{cite book|title=Birds of Botswana |first1=Peter|last1=Hancock|first2=Ingrid|last2=Weiersbye|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H-d0CgAAQBAJ&pg=PA158|publisher=[[Princeton University Press]]|location=Princeton, NJ|page=158|date=24 November 2015|access-date=11 May 2016|isbn=978-1-4008-7417-0}}</ref> The name of this bird commemorates the English naturalist [[William John Burchell]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Beolens|first=Bo|title=Whose Bird? Men and Women Commemorated in the Common Names of Birds|year=2003|publisher=Christopher Helm|location=London|page=72|author2=Watkins, Michael|isbn=978-0-7136-6647-2}}</ref>

Native to Africa, Burchell's courser is a small, [[Diurnality|diurnal]], and terrestrial bird that lives in the western parts of southern [[Africa]].<ref name="Birdlife" /> Although classed as [[wader]]s, these are [[bird]]s of dry open country, preferably semi-desert, where they typically hunt their [[insect]] prey<ref name="Birdlife">{{cite web|title=Species factsheet: Cursorius rufus|first1=J.|last1=Ekstrom|first2=S.|last2=Butchart|first3=L.|last3=Malpas|url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=3184|publisher=[[BirdLife International]]|date=2016|access-date=11 May 2016}}</ref> (usually [[Hodotermitidae|Harvester Termites]])<ref name="Alive">{{cite web|title=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive: Burchell's Courser (Cursorius rufus)|first1=G.L.|last1=Maclean|first2=G.M.|last2=Kirwan|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/burchells-courser-cursorius-rufus|publisher=Lynx Edicions|location=Barcelona, Spain|date=2016|access-date=11 May 2016}}</ref> by running on the ground. It principally feeds off of [[insects]] and lives in open, short [[grasslands]] and burnt [[veld]].<ref name=Birdlife/> It grows up to {{Convert|22|cm|abbr=on}} and on average weighs about {{Convert|75|g|abbr=on}} as an adult.<ref name=Alive/>

==Description==
[[File:Cursorius rufus - Etosha 2014.jpg|thumb|left|Close-up of ''C. rufus''|alt=]]
Burchell's courser has a graceful figure and an upright posture. It has a blue-grey hind crown which is mainly how it differs from the similar [[Temminck's courser]].<ref name="Alive" /> It also has a horizontal black band demarcating a white underbelly, and an overall pale [[rufous]] colour.<ref name="Alive" /> Its face features white [[Supercilium|supercilia]] above black eye lines, all ending at the back of the neck to form a double V.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last1=Marchant|first1=John|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aJpGDwAAQBAJ&q=burchell%27s+courser|title=Shorebirds|last2=Hayman|first2=Peter|last3=Prater|first3=Tony|date=2010-09-30|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4081-3515-0|language=en}}</ref> In terms of feathers, its secondaries are mostly white with a black underwing and brown to grey [[Covert feather|coverts]].<ref name=":0" /> Typical of coursers, its bill is long, arched down, and of a dark colour like its eyes.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4yDsDwAAQBAJ&q=why+is+burchell%27s+courser+rufus+local+nomad&pg=PA212|title=The Birds of Africa: Volume II|date=2020-05-29|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4729-8640-5|language=en}}</ref> Similar to the underbelly, the legs are of a distinctive creamy white.<ref name=":0" /><ref name="Alive" /> While flying, the Burchell's courser's feet extend past its tail.<ref name=":0" /> 

Females and males look similar, while juveniles lack the rufous colouring, the facial stripes, and the grey hind crown of the adults.<ref name=":1" /> They also are [[Mottle|mottled]], with black and beige barring.<ref name="Alive" /><ref name=":1" /> Overall, the Burchell's courser, with its camouflage appearance, blends in with the landscape making it challenging to spot.

==Taxonomy==
Burchell's courser belongs to the order [[Charadriiformes]] which mainly refers to birds living near water.<ref>{{Cite web|title=ITIS Standard Report Page: Cursorius rufus|url=https://www.itis.gov/servlet/SingleRpt/SingleRpt?search_topic=TSN&search_value=559605#null|access-date=2020-10-12|website=www.itis.gov}}</ref> Characterized by a curved beak and nostrils located at the base of the nose, the Burchell's courser is part of the [[Glareolidae]] family, specifically belonging to the [[courser]] group. Its genus, ''[[Cursorius]],'' contains five species, all of which live in the arid parts of the [[Old World]] and display distinctive features such as long legs and short wings.<ref name=":5">{{Cite web|title=Pratincoles and Coursers (Glareolidae) {{!}} Encyclopedia.com|url=https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/pratincoles-and-coursers-glareolidae#Burchells_courser|access-date=2020-10-12|website=www.encyclopedia.com}}</ref> 

Burchell's courser and its close relative, ''[[Cream-coloured courser|C. cursor]]'', have at times been referred to as the same species.<ref name=":0" /><ref name="Alive" /> However, although they have a direct common ancestor, they should not be considered as such.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Clancey|first=P. A.|date=1986-01-01|title=Endemicity in the Southern African Avifauna|url=https://journals.co.za/content/admn/13/20/AJA0012723X_2158?fromSearch=true|journal=Durban Museum Novitates|language=en|volume=13|issue=20|pages=245–284|issn=0012-723X}}</ref> ''C. rufus'' is [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]] and does not have any subspecies.<ref name="Alive" />

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Burchell's Courser.jpg|thumb|''C. rufus'' in a gravelly desert|alt=]]
[[Endemic]] to Africa, Burchell's courser prefers the warm and dry areas of [[Southern Africa]].<ref name="Hancock" /> Specifically, it is found in most of [[South Africa]] and [[Namibia]], and in parts of [[Angola]] (Southwest) and [[Botswana]]<ref>{{Cite journal|last=WILSON|first=J.R.|date=1983|title=The Avifauna of the Lobatse Area, South-East Botswana|journal=Botswana Notes and Records|volume=15|pages=59–74|jstor=40979681|issn=0525-5090}}</ref> ([[Kalahari Basin]]).<ref name="Alive" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite web|last=International)|first=BirdLife International (BirdLife|date=2016-10-01|title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Cursorius rufus|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|access-date=2020-10-12|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref> Its distribution range occasionally coincides with that of the [[Temminck's courser]].<ref name=":1" /> 

Burchell's courser inhabits [[Desert|deserts]] or semi deserts with gravel rather than just sand, but also open grasslands with short grass, and sparse, burnt, or overgrazed vegetation.<ref name="Alive" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> It is often referred as locally nomad based on its movements at the landscape level, possibly travelling due to fluctuations in rainfall.<ref name="Alive" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last=Dean|first=W. R. J.|date=1997|title=The Distribution and Biology of Nomadic Birds in the Karoo, South Africa|journal=Journal of Biogeography|volume=24|issue=6|pages=769–779|doi=10.1046/j.1365-2699.1997.00163.x|jstor=2846114|issn=0305-0270}}</ref><ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last=Maclean|first=G. L.|date=2008-04-03|title=The Breeding Biology and Behaviour of the Doublebanded Courser ''Rhznoptzlus Afrzcnus'' (Temminck)|url=https://onlinelibrary-wiley-com.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1967.tb00025.x?sid=worldcat.org|journal=Ibis|volume=109|issue=4|pages=556–569|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1967.tb00025.x|issn=0019-1019|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Although it is classified as [[Least-concern species|least concern]], Its population is declining, potentially due to its habitat loss on arable lands, inadequate farming practices such as intense [[irrigation]] and overuse of [[Pesticide|pesticides]], and other anthropogenic disturbances.<ref name="Hancock" /><ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hauptfleisch|first1=Morgan L.|last2=Avenant|first2=Nico L.|last3=Tsowaseb|first3=Alton|date=2013|title=Aircraft-Wildlife Collisions at Two Major Namibian Airports from 2006–2010|url=https://doi-org.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/10.3957/056.043.0208|journal=South African Journal of Wildlife Research|volume=43|issue=2|pages=177–184|doi=10.3957/056.043.0208|s2cid=129638419|issn=0379-4369|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The adults have developed a [[Distraction display|diversionary display]] as a strategy against predators. They perform "displacement [[brooding]]" to distract any potential threats from the nesting area.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":6" /> Even though the chicks are considered [[Precociality|precocial]],<ref name=":5" /> they lack the heat-loss mechanisms present in the adults to survive the hot climate, and thus need shade to avoid overheating.<ref name=":6" /> 

===Voice=== 
{{Birdsong
 | url = https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Cursorius-rufus
 | species = Cursorius rufus
 }} 
Burchell's courser often prefers to run rather than fly, and will most likely take to the air only when the disturbance is significant.<ref name=":5" /> Often referred to as inconspicuous, this bird is mostly quiet on the ground, but becomes vocal when flying, producing a loud [[flight call]].<ref name=":0" /> Thus far, three types of vocalizations it produces have been identified: one throaty "chuk" as it launches in the air, a few "chuk chuk" grunts while flying, and a [[contact call]] consisting of a "kwirrt-kwirrt" or a "kok-kok-kwich".<ref name="Alive" /><ref name=":1" /> If threatened by a predator, the Burchell's courser may run away from its nest, emitting an [[alarm call]] in order to divert the predator.<ref name=":6" />

===Feeding===
Burchell's courser is predominantly an [[insectivore]].<ref name="Alive" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Kok|first1=O. B.|last2=Kok|first2=A. C.|date=2002-04-01|title=Diet of three courser species in an open grassland habitat, central South Africa|url=https://journals.co.za/content/wild/32/1/EJC117141|journal=South African Journal of Wildlife Research|language=en|volume=32|issue=1|pages=39–42|issn=2410-7220}}</ref> It uses its long bill to forage the ground and dig up insects. This bird also runs to catch its prey.<ref name="Alive" /> It has been found to frequently eat from the [[Beetle|Coleoptera]] group, especially [[curculionidae]] beetles as its main food source.<ref name=":4" /> However, the Burchell's courser tends to have a preference for [[harvester termites]] (''Hodotermes'' ''mossambicus'') when available, which can constitute over 50% of its diet.<ref name=":4" /> It almost never chooses seeds as a source of nutrition.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":4" /> The yearly frequency of rainfalls affects the food availability and quality, which is the main reason the Burchell's courser travels around.<ref name=":3" />

===Breeding===
Burchell's coursers may occur in small [[Flock (birds)|flocks]] but are mostly solitary, [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] birds.<ref name="Alive" /><ref name=":1" /> They have developed an all-year-round breeding strategy due to their nomadic movements,<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":6" /> although they often breed just before the [[wet season]] between July and December.  

With a small [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] size, the Burchell's courser only lays two eggs directly on the ground, sometimes surrounded by stools of antelopes, small rocks, or dried up [[organic matter]].<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":5" /> The eggs have an oval shape, and look black from afar, but they are actually of a buff colour with many dark markings.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":5" /> There is a lot of uncertainty when it comes to the [[Egg incubation|hatching]] and [[Fledge|fledging]] times, but it is believed that both parents participate in the incubation period.<ref name=":1" />
